questions.q: Which command marks a node as unschedulable but does NOT evict any running pods?
questions.explanation: kubectl cordon marks the node as SchedulingDisabled, preventing new pods from landing there, but leaves existing pods running. kubectl drain cordons the node AND evicts all pods.

questions.q: Why must you specify --ignore-daemonsets when draining a node that runs daemonsets?
options: DaemonSet pods cannot run on cordoned nodes
DaemonSet pods are managed by kubelet directly
DaemonSet pods are automatically recreated on the same node by the DaemonSet controller, leading to an infinite drain loop
questions.explanation: DaemonSet pods run on all nodes (or a subset) by default. If you evict a DaemonSet pod, the DaemonSet controller will immediately recreate it on the same node, which blocks kubectl drain from completing. Thus, you must ignore them.

questions.q: When you uncordon a node, pods that were previously evicted from it will automatically move back.
questions.explanation: False. kubectl uncordon only allows new pods to be scheduled on the node. Existing/evicted pods that were rescheduled to other nodes stay where they are; they are not automatically moved back.
